t: Ambit Energy's Free Energy Program, Explained
q: Is Ambit Energy's Free Energy program actually free electricity?
a: It is a referral reward, not a free electricity plan. You still choose a normal Ambit plan and pay your bill. Once you refer enough active customers, Ambit credits the energy (kWh) portion of a month of your service. Delivery charges and taxes are generally not part of the credit.

t: Ambit Energy's Free Energy Program, Explained
q: How do you earn the free month from Ambit?
a: You refer other customers who enroll in their own Ambit plan and stay active and in good standing. Once you have enough active referred accounts and your own account is current, you qualify for the Free Energy credit. The exact number of referrals required can change, so confirm current terms with Ambit or your consultant.

t: Ambit Energy's Free Energy Program, Explained
q: What does the Free Energy credit cover?
a: The credit applies to the energy charges for the kilowatt-hours you used that month. It generally does not cover the utility (TDU) delivery charges that move power to your home, or state and local taxes and fees, because those are pass-through costs on every Texas bill.

t: Ambit Energy's Free Energy Program, Explained
q: How do I check the real rate before signing up?
a: Read the Electricity Facts Label (EFL) for the plan at your ZIP code. It shows the average price per kWh at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh, the split between energy and delivery charges, the contract term, any early-termination fee, and whether the rate is fixed or variable. Energy Direct can compare EFLs by your address with you.

q: How do I find out how many kWh my home uses?
a: Check any recent electricity bill or your provider's online account. Each statement lists the kWh used that month. Compare a summer bill and a winter bill to see your usage range.

t: Average Home Electricity Usage in Texas
q: Why is the advertised electricity rate different from what I actually pay?
a: Advertised rates are usually quoted at 1,000 kWh. Base charges, bill credits, and tiered pricing change your effective rate at other usage levels. Always confirm a plan's price at your real usage on its Electricity Facts Label (EFL).

t: Average Home Electricity Usage in Texas
q: Do Texas homes really use more electricity than average?
a: Texas homes tend to land on the higher side nationally, largely because of heavy air-conditioning use during long, hot summers. Your own usage still depends on home size, whether you have gas or all-electric heating, and your household habits.

t: Average Home Electricity Usage in Texas

Your monthly bill is mostly the total kWh your home used, multiplied by your rate, plus delivery charges. So your usage and your rate work together. Neither one tells the whole story alone.

q: What is a bill-credit electricity plan?
a: It is a plan that charges a per-kWh energy rate and adds a flat dollar credit to your bill when your monthly usage falls within a defined range, often at or above 1,000 kWh. The credit lowers your effective rate when you qualify.

q: Why does the advertised rate on a bill-credit plan look so low?
a: The advertised average price is usually quoted at exactly 1,000 kWh, where the credit has the most impact. At other usage levels, especially below the threshold, the real price can be much higher. Always check the average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh on the Electricity Facts Label.

q: What happens if I use less than the bill-credit threshold?
a: If your usage falls below the credit threshold for a month, you typically lose the entire credit and pay the plan's full energy rate on every kWh. A mild Texas spring or fall month can easily trigger this.

q: Are bill-credit plans a bad deal?
a: No. For a household with steady usage that consistently lands inside the credit window, a bill-credit plan can be one of the cheapest options available. They become a problem only when your real usage pattern does not match the plan's threshold.

q: How do I know if a bill-credit plan fits my home?
a: Pull your last 12 months of kWh usage and check how many months would have hit the credit range, then compare the live EFL for the plans at your address. Energy Direct can compare plans by ZIP with you, and Ambit handles the switch once you choose.

q: What a bill-credit plan is
a: A bill-credit plan charges you a per-kWh energy rate like any other plan, but it also gives you a flat dollar credit on your bill when your usage for the month falls inside a defined range. The credit is the marketing hook. The provider sets the energy rate a little higher than it otherwise would, then "hands back" a chunk of money each m

q: What a bill-credit plan is
a: A typical structure looks like this: a credit applies when your monthly usage lands between two numbers, for example between 1,000 kWh and 2,000 kWh. Use inside that window and the credit drops your effective rate. Use outside it and the credit can disappear entirely.

q: Why providers design plans this way
a: Bill credits let a provider advertise a very low "average price" at one specific usage level. In Texas, every plan must publish its average price per kWh at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh on the Electricity Facts Label (EFL). A bill credit can make the 1,000 kWh number look dramatically low while the 500 kWh number stays high, because the cred

q: Why providers design plans this way
a: That is not necessarily a bad thing. It just means the headline rate is tuned to a specific usage profile, and you have to ask whether that profile is actually yours.

q: The core trap
a: The advertised "average price" on a bill-credit plan is usually quoted at 1,000 kWh. If your home does not use right around 1,000 kWh in a given month, you may never see that price, and a low-usage month can leave you paying a high raw energy rate with no credit at all.

q: 1. Falling below the credit threshold
a: This is the big one. Many bill credits only apply when you use at or above a minimum, often 1,000 kWh. In Texas, a mild spring or fall month can easily drop a home under that line. When it does, you lose the entire credit and pay the full, higher energy rate on every kWh. A plan that looked cheap in July can be expensive in March.

q: 2. Climbing above the upper band
a: Some plans only give the credit within a range and remove it once you exceed the top of that band. A hot August with the AC running hard can push a Texas home past the ceiling, and the credit you were counting on vanishes in the very month your bill is highest.

q: 3. The "low usage penalty" feeling
a: Because the energy rate is set higher to fund the credit, low-usage months on a bill-credit plan often cost more than they would on a simple fixed-rate plan. If you travel, have a small apartment, or a second property that sits empty part of the year, this can quietly add up.

q: 4. Confusing the credit with a discount
a: A bill credit is a flat dollar amount, not a percentage off. It does not scale with your usage. The more you understand it as "a fixed amount I only get if I hit the threshold," the easier it is to judge honestly.

q: When a bill-credit plan is actually a good deal
a: Bill-credit plans are not a gimmick to avoid across the board. They genuinely shine for the right household: Steady, predictable usage — if your home reliably uses an amount that lands inside the credit window most months, you capture the credit consistently and the effective rate can be excellent. Larger homes — a bigger house with year-

a: Households that can shift a little usage — if you are close to a threshold, you may be able to nudge usage to stay on the right side of it, though that is a fragile way to save. The deciding factor is your own usage pattern across the whole year, not just one good month.

q: Do I have to choose a provider in Texas?
a: If you live in a deregulated area, you choose your retail provider. If you are served by a municipal utility or electric cooperative, you do not shop. Entering your service address tells you which situation applies.

q: Will switching electricity providers interrupt my power?
a: No. Your Transmission and Distribution Utility keeps delivering electricity on the same wires and meter. Only the company that bills you changes, and the switch is handled electronically with no technician visit.

q: What is the difference between my TDU and my retail provider?
a: The TDU owns the poles, wires, and meter and is fixed by your address. The retail provider is the company you choose to buy power from. Your bill combines the TDU's regulated delivery charge with your provider's energy rate.

t: What Happens When Your Texas Electricity Contract Expires
q: What happens when my Texas electricity contract expires?
a: Your power is not shut off. If you do nothing, your service continues on a default month-to-month holdover rate set by your provider. That rate is usually variable and often higher than the fixed rate you had, so it pays to compare and sign a new plan before your contract ends.

t: What Happens When Your Texas Electricity Contract Expires
q: Will my electricity get shut off if my contract ends?
a: No. When a Texas electricity contract expires you keep your service. You simply roll onto a default month-to-month plan until you choose a new contract, so there is no interruption to your power.

t: What Happens When Your Texas Electricity Contract Expires
q: Can I switch electricity providers after my contract expires without a fee?
a: Yes. Once your contract term has ended you are month to month and can switch any time with no early termination fee. Early termination fees only apply if you cancel before your term is up.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Is a fixed-rate or variable-rate plan cheaper in Texas?
a: Neither is always cheaper. Fixed-rate plans save you money when market rates rise because your price is locked in. Variable-rate plans save you money when rates fall, but they expose you to spikes. The right choice depends on market conditions and your risk tolerance.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Do variable-rate plans have an early-termination fee?
a: Usually not. Most variable-rate plans let you leave at any time with no early-termination fee, which is part of why they appeal to people who may move soon. Fixed-rate plans typically charge $150 to $300 to cancel early.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: What is the Electricity Facts Label (EFL) and why does it matter?
a: The EFL is a standardized disclosure that shows a plan's average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh of usage, plus base charges and fees. Comparing the EFL at your actual usage level is the only fair way to compare plans, since the headline rate alone can be misleading.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: How long should I stay on a plan before switching?
a: Plan to stay at least 6 months. Constantly switching for a slightly lower rate wastes time and can trigger fees or hidden costs. Pick a plan that fits your budget and usage, then let it run.

q: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
a: If you are shopping for electricity in Texas, you have probably seen two main options: fixed-rate and variable-rate plans. Both have pros and cons, and which one saves you the most money depends entirely on your goals and how you use electricity. In this guide, we break down exactly how each plan works, show you real examples, and help yo

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Fixed-rate plans
a: With a fixed-rate plan, your electricity rate is locked in for the entire contract term, usually 6, 12, 24, or 36 months. No matter what happens to the energy market, your rate stays the same. Example: You sign up for a 12-month fixed plan at 12¢ per kWh. In month 6, market rates jump to 18¢ per kWh. Your rate? Still 12¢.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Variable-rate plans
a: With a variable-rate plan, your electricity rate changes monthly based on market conditions. When wholesale prices go down, your rate goes down. When prices go up, so does your rate. Example: You sign up for a variable plan at 11¢ per kWh in January. By June, market rates drop to 9¢ per kWh, and your rate follows. By November, rates jump

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Side-by-side comparison
a: * Base charges and usage still apply. Your total bill = (kWh used × rate) + base charge.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Scenario 1: market rates stay stable
a: Your usage: 1,000 kWh/month  ·  Contract length: 12 months Fixed plan: 12¢/kWh locked in for 12 months = $120/month × 12 = $1,440/year Variable plan: Average 12¢/kWh over the year = $120/month × 12 = $1,440/year Winner: Tie. Both cost the same.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Scenario 2: market rates rise (volatility)
a: Your usage: 1,000 kWh/month  ·  Contract length: 12 months Fixed plan: 12¢/kWh locked in = $120/month × 12 = $1,440/year Variable plan: Starts at 11¢, rises to average 14¢ by end of year = ~$130/month average × 12 = $1,560/year Winner: Fixed-rate plan saves about $120/year.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Scenario 3: market rates fall (favorable market)
a: Your usage: 1,000 kWh/month  ·  Contract length: 12 months Fixed plan: 12¢/kWh locked in = $120/month × 12 = $1,440/year Variable plan: Starts at 12¢, drops to average 9¢ by mid-year = ~$105/month average × 12 = $1,260/year Winner: Variable-rate plan saves about $180/year.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Key insight
a: Fixed-rate plans protect you when markets are rising. Variable-rate plans reward you when markets are falling, but expose you to spikes. Neither is inherently better. It depends on market conditions and your risk tolerance.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: Choose a fixed-rate plan if:
a: You want budget predictability and peace of mind You are risk-averse and do not want rate surprises You are planning to stay in your home for 2 or more years Market conditions are uncertain or volatile You have a tight monthly budget and cannot absorb bill spikes

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?

a: The EFL shows the average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh usage levels. Use this to compare plans fairly, not just the headline rate.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: 2. Factor in your usage pattern
a: A plan that is great for a 1,000 kWh/month user might be expensive for a 2,000 kWh/month user. Your EFL usage tier matters.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: 3. Watch for base charges and fees
a: Some plans charge a monthly base fee ($10 to $20) that applies even if you use no electricity. This is part of your true cost, so check the EFL.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: 4. Check contract terms and early-termination fees
a: Fixed plans often have $150 to $300 early-termination fees. If you might move, a variable plan with no fee might be smarter.

t: Fixed-Rate vs Variable-Rate Electricity Plans in Texas: Which Saves More?
q: 5. Do not switch too often
a: Constantly switching for a slightly lower rate wastes time and can be penny-wise, pound-foolish. Stick with a plan for at least 6 months.

a: Fixed-rate plans are best for budget certainty and protection against market spikes. Variable-rate plans offer flexibility and upside potential when rates fall, but with market risk. q: Are free nights and weekends electricity plans really free?
a: The energy charge is waived only during the plan's free window, such as overnight hours or weekends. Delivery charges and any monthly base fee can still apply, and providers typically set the paid-hour rate higher to make up for the free energy. Check the plan's Electricity Facts Label for the exact terms.

t: Do "Free Nights & Weekends" Electricity Plans Actually Save Money?
q: Who saves money on a free nights or free weekends plan?
a: Households that use most of their electricity during the free window. That includes people who are away during the day, those who can shift big loads like laundry, dishwashing, or EV charging to off-peak hours, and weekend-heavy homes on a free weekends plan.

t: Do "Free Nights & Weekends" Electricity Plans Actually Save Money?
q: Who tends to pay more on these plans?
a: Households that use a lot of power during the paid hours. Remote workers, retirees, and families home all day often run their heaviest usage during the expensive daytime window, and Texas summer afternoon air conditioning frequently falls inside the paid window where the rate is highest.

t: Do "Free Nights & Weekends" Electricity Plans Actually Save Money?

a: Look at when you use the most electricity. If roughly half or more of your usage already falls inside the plan's free window, it is worth a serious look. If most of your use is during the day, a competitive fixed-rate plan usually wins. Read the EFL for your ZIP and compare the all-in price at your usage level.

t: 100% Green, Renewable Electricity Plans in Texas
q: What does a 100% renewable electricity plan in Texas mean?
a: It means your retail provider buys and retires enough Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) to match all the electricity you use. For every unit of clean wind or solar power added to the Texas grid, a REC is created, and a 100% renewable plan pairs your usage one for one with that renewable generation.

t: 100% Green, Renewable Electricity Plans in Texas
q: Do green energy plans cost more in Texas?
a: Not necessarily. Because Texas is the national leader in wind power and a fast-growing solar producer, renewable plans are often priced in line with standard plans and are sometimes the most competitive option. Always read the live Electricity Facts Label (EFL) and compare at your service address to see the real price.

t: 100% Green, Renewable Electricity Plans in Texas
q: Is the electricity from a renewable plan different from regular power?
a: The power coming into your home is the same grid mix as everyone else, and reliability is identical. The difference is that a renewable plan matches your usage to clean generation through retired RECs, so the energy you buy is tied to wind and solar instead of an average grid mix.

t: 100% Green, Renewable Electricity Plans in Texas
q: What "100% renewable" actually means
a: When you flip a light switch, you cannot trace the exact electrons coming down the wire. The grid is a shared pool where power from every source, wind, solar, natural gas, coal, and nuclear, mixes together. So a 100% renewable plan does not deliver special "green" electricity to your house. Instead, it works through a tracking system.

t: 100% Green, Renewable Electricity Plans in Texas
q: What "100% renewable" actually means
a: For every megawatt-hour of clean power a wind or solar farm puts onto the Texas grid, a Renewable Energy Certificate (REC) is created. A REC is the official proof that one unit of renewable generation happened. When your retail provider sells you a 100% renewable plan, it buys and retires enough RECs to match all the electricity you use.

t: How to Read Your Texas Electricity Bill
q: What is the difference between the energy charge and TDU charges?
a: The energy charge is the electricity you bought, priced by your retail provider, and is the part shopping can lower. TDU charges are the regulated delivery fees from the utility that owns the wires; they are the same for every provider and switching does not change them.

t: How to Read Your Texas Electricity Bill
q: Can I reduce my TDU delivery charges by switching providers?
a: No. Delivery charges are set by your utility and approved by the Public Utility Commission of Texas, so they are identical no matter who you buy energy from. The way to lower your bill is a better energy rate and using less.

t: How to Read Your Texas Electricity Bill
q: How do I find my real price per kWh?
a: Divide your total bill by the kilowatt-hours you used that month. That all-in average includes the energy charge, base fees, any credits, and delivery, so it is far more honest than the advertised headline rate.

t: How to Read Your Texas Electricity Bill
q: Why does a plan with a low rate sometimes cost more?
a: Usually a bill credit or minimum-usage fee. The low rate may only apply inside a narrow usage window; outside it the credit disappears or a penalty applies, and your effective price rises. Check the Electricity Facts Label at the usage tier closest to your home.

t: Minimum-Usage Fees on Texas Electricity Plans, Explained
q: What is a minimum-usage fee on a Texas electricity plan?
a: A minimum-usage fee is a flat charge a retail electric provider adds to your bill when your monthly electricity use falls below a set threshold, often around 1,000 kWh. If you use more than the threshold, the fee is usually waived. The exact threshold and fee amount are set by each plan and disclosed on the Electricity Facts Label (EFL).

a: Know your typical monthly kWh usage, then choose a plan that matches it. If you regularly use less than 1,000 kWh, look for plans with no minimum-usage fee. Always read the EFL and compare the average price at 500 kWh and 1,000 kWh before enrolling, since a large gap signals a plan built for heavy users.

q: What is a minimum-usage fee?
a: It is essentially the opposite of a volume discount. Instead of rewarding heavy use, the plan penalizes light use. Providers build these fees into plans that are priced to look attractive at higher usage levels, so a household that uses very little ends up paying more per kilowatt-hour than the headline rate suggests.

a: This is why two months on the exact same plan can feel completely different. A heavy summer month with the air conditioning running may clear the threshold and avoid the fee, while a mild spring month with the windows open falls short and triggers it.

t: Setting Up Electricity at a New Texas Address
q: How far in advance should I set up electricity before moving in?
a: Enrolling 1 to 3 business days before your move-in date is usually enough for service to start on time. Many providers also offer same-day or next-day connection if you need it, sometimes with an added TDU connection fee.

t: Setting Up Electricity at a New Texas Address
q: Do I have to cancel my old electricity service when I move?
a: Yes. Setting up service at a new address does not automatically end service at your old one. Schedule a disconnect at the old home, ideally for the day after you move out, so you are not billed for a place you have left.

t: Setting Up Electricity at a New Texas Address
q: Will I need a deposit to start electricity service in Texas?
a: It depends on the provider and your credit. Deposits are common, are typically refundable, and some plans or prepaid options let you avoid one. Check the plan terms or ask Energy Direct about options that fit your situation.

t: Setting Up Electricity at a New Texas Address
q: Can I keep my current provider when I move within Texas?
a: Often yes, if your provider serves your new address, but a move is a good time to re-compare. Your new address may fall under a different TDU with different plans, so the best deal at your old home may not be the best one at the new one. Always check the live EFL for your new ZIP.

t: Power Outage? Who to Call in Texas
q: Who do I call to report a power outage in Texas?
a: Call your Transmission and Distribution Utility (TDU), not your retail electric provider. The TDU owns the poles and wires and runs the repair crews. Major Texas TDUs are Oncor, CenterPoint Energy, AEP Texas, and TNMP, each with its own 24/7 outage line.

t: Power Outage? Who to Call in Texas
q: Why can't my electricity provider fix my outage?
a: In deregulated Texas, your retail provider only handles your rate, billing, and account. They do not own power lines or repair crews. Restoring power is the job of your TDU, the regulated utility that physically delivers electricity to your home.

t: Power Outage? Who to Call in Texas
q: How do I know which TDU serves my address?
a: Your TDU is listed on your electric bill and owns the meter on your home. It is determined by your location, not your provider. In Texas, the major TDUs are Oncor (Dallas-Fort Worth and North/West Texas), CenterPoint (Houston), AEP Texas (Corpus Christi and the Coastal Bend), and TNMP.

t: Power Outage? Who to Call in Texas
q: What should I do if I see a downed power line?
a: Treat every downed line as live and dangerous, even if it looks dead. Stay well back, keep others away, and report it to your TDU immediately. If a line is sparking, on fire, or has fallen on an occupied vehicle, call 911 first.

a: It can, because air conditioning is usually the largest part of a Texas summer bill and the thermostat controls it. q: What temperature should I set my thermostat in a Texas summer?

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 1. Install and program a smart thermostat
a: Set a sensible summer schedule, let it ease the temperature up while you are out, and check the usage report each week. This is the move that touches your largest load, so it goes first.

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 2. Service your AC and change the filter
a: A dirty filter or a low-on-refrigerant system makes the AC work harder for the same cooling. Change the filter on schedule, keep the outdoor condenser clear of leaves and grass, and have the system checked before peak summer. A well-maintained unit cools faster and shuts off sooner.

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 3. Seal the leaks and add shade
a: Cooled air that escapes through gaps around doors, windows, and attic hatches is air you paid to cool. Weatherstrip and caulk the obvious leaks. Close blinds on the sun-facing side during the afternoon. In a Texas summer, blocking direct sun through windows takes a real load off the AC.

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 4. Switch to LED lighting
a: LED bulbs use a fraction of the electricity of old incandescent bulbs and last far longer. They also give off much less heat, which matters in summer because every bit of waste heat indoors is something your AC then has to remove.

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 5. Tame the water heater and the laundry
a: The water heater is often the second-largest user after the AC. Wash clothes in cold water when you can, run full loads, and clean the dryer lint trap every time. If your water heater has an adjustable thermostat set higher than you need, lowering it a little trims standby cost without anyone noticing.

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 6. Unplug the always-on "phantom" loads
a: Game consoles, chargers, cable boxes, and older electronics draw power even when they look off. Individually each is small, but together they run twenty-four hours a day, every day. A smart power strip or simply unplugging the worst offenders quietly removes that baseline draw.

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: 7. Shift heavy use to off-peak hours
a: Some Texas plans charge different prices at different times of day, and even on a flat plan the grid is most strained on hot late afternoons. Running the dishwasher, laundry, and pool pump later in the evening eases your usage off the most expensive part of the day. Whether this saves you money depends on your specific plan, which brings

t: Smart Thermostats and 7 Ways to Cut Your Texas Electric Bill
q: Key insight
a: Using less electricity only lowers your bill if your rate is fair to begin with. The habits above cut how many kilowatt-hours you use; your plan decides what each one costs. Both have to be right.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: Will I get credit for the excess power my solar panels generate?
a: On a solar buyback plan, yes. The surplus power your panels send back to the grid is credited on your bill. The exact value is set by the plan you choose, and a guaranteed 1:1 credit is not standard, so compare the buyback terms on the Electricity Facts Label before you enroll.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: Do I need solar panels already installed to get a solar buyback plan?
a: Yes. Solar buyback plans are built for homes that already have working panels. You can enter your ZIP and enroll in a plan even if your panels are already up and running.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: Does Energy Direct install the solar panels?
a: No. Energy Direct sells the electricity plan, not the panels. Your panels and inverter are installed by a separate solar installer of your choice. We help you find and enroll in a retail plan that credits the extra power those panels make.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: When it tends to make sense
a: You have roof space with good sun exposure and panels that already export surplus power. Your home regularly sends meaningful excess back to the grid rather than consuming all it makes. You plan to stay in the home long enough to benefit from the credits. Your address and TDU support a buyback or net-metering-style plan.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: When it may matter less
a: Your usage is high enough that you consume nearly all your solar output, leaving little to export. Your roof is heavily shaded or poorly oriented, so production is low. A plan's monthly fees or low buyback rate cancel out most of the credit value.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: Tips for getting the most from a buyback plan
a: Compare how exports are credited, not just the headline rate. A slightly higher rate you pay can still net out better if the buyback credit is stronger. Consider pairing solar with battery storage. Storing surplus for evening use can beat exporting it at a reduced buyback rate, depending on your plan.

t: Solar Buyback Plans in Texas: How Bill Credits Work
q: Tips for getting the most from a buyback plan
a: Shift heavy loads into daylight hours. Running the dishwasher, laundry, or EV charging while the sun is up uses your own solar before it is exported. Watch your production. Most solar systems include an app that shows real-time generation, which helps you time usage and confirm your panels are performing.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Will my credit be affected if I switch electricity providers in Texas?
a: No. Switching electricity providers is not a credit inquiry and will not affect your credit score.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Do I need to cancel my old provider when I switch?
a: No. Your old provider is automatically notified by the utility (TDU). The switch is handled on the back end.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Will I lose service during the switch?
a: No. Your utility keeps service continuous during a provider switch. There is zero downtime.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Can I switch back if I don't like the new plan?
a: Yes, but check the new contract's early-termination fee first. You can always switch again once your contract ends, typically after 12 months.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Are there any hidden fees to switch electricity providers in Texas?
a: No. Switching providers in Texas is completely free. The only potential fee is an early-termination fee from your current provider if you are still under contract.

q: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
a: Thinking about switching your electricity provider in Texas? Good news: it is easy, it is free, and you will not lose power during the switch. Here is everything you need to know.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: The myth: "I'll lose power when I switch"
a: False. This is the number-one question we get, and the answer is always no. Here is why: Your local utility (TDU) — Oncor, CenterPoint, or AEP — owns the poles and wires. That utility keeps delivering electricity 24/7 no matter who you buy it from. Your new provider coordinates the switch, so there is zero downtime.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas

a: It is like changing banks: your money does not disappear during the transfer, and you never lose access.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas

a: Enter your service address and see every plan available there. Look for the lowest rate at your typical usage level.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Step 2 — Enroll (about 2 minutes)
a: Choose a plan and provide basic info. Most providers let you enroll online in under five minutes.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Step 3 — Confirmation (24 to 48 hours)
a: Your new provider submits the switch request to your utility. You receive a confirmation email with your start date.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Step 4 — Automatic switch (15 to 30 days)
a: Your utility coordinates with your old and new providers. The switch happens automatically on your start date. No outage. No manual cancellation needed.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Step 5 — New bills
a: Your new provider sends the next bill. You may get one final bill from your old provider for any remaining usage.

t: How to Switch Electricity Providers in Texas
q: Total time, start to switch: 15 to 30 days
a: The entire process is automatic. Your new provider handles coordination with your utility, so you do not have to call anyone or cancel anything manually.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: What is a TDU in Texas?
a: TDU stands for Transmission and Distribution Utility. It is the company that owns the poles, wires, and meter that physically deliver electricity to your home and restore power after outages. You do not choose your TDU; it is assigned by location. Major Texas TDUs include Oncor, CenterPoint Energy, AEP Texas, and TNMP.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: Is the TDU delivery charge a hidden fee or a scam?
a: No. The TDU delivery charge is a legitimate, regulated, pass-through cost approved by the Public Utility Commission of Texas. Every Texas customer on a competitive plan pays it, and it is the same regardless of which retail provider you choose.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: Can I shop around for a cheaper TDU delivery charge?
a: No. Your TDU is assigned by location and cannot be changed by switching providers, so the delivery charge is identical for everyone in your territory. What you can shop for is the energy rate and plan terms, which is where choosing the right plan saves money.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: How do I find my current TDU delivery charge?
a: Check the Electricity Facts Label (EFL) for any plan in your area, which shows the all-in average price including delivery charges, or look up your TDU's published delivery schedule. You can also give your ZIP or service address to Energy Direct and we will pull the live, address-specific numbers.

q: What is a TDU?
a: TDU stands for Transmission and Distribution Utility . t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: What does a TDU delivery charge actually pay for?
a: Your energy charge pays for the electricity itself. Your delivery charge pays to move that electricity from the power plant, across high-voltage transmission lines, down through the local distribution wires, and into your meter. It also covers maintaining that equipment and keeping the lights on after outages.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: What does a TDU delivery charge actually pay for?
a: Every Texas customer on a competitive plan pays a delivery charge. It is not an optional add-on and it is not a fee your provider invented. It is a regulated, pass-through cost set by your TDU and approved by the Public Utility Commission of Texas (PUCT).

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: Key insight
a: Your TDU delivery charge is the same no matter which retail provider you choose. Two neighbors on the same TDU pay identical delivery rates. The part that actually differs between providers is the energy charge.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: How TDU charges are structured
a: TDU delivery charges almost always come in two parts: A fixed monthly charge — a flat amount you pay every billing cycle just for being connected, regardless of how much power you use. A per-kWh delivery charge — an amount added for every kilowatt-hour you consume, on top of your provider's energy rate.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: How TDU charges are structured
a: These rates are published by each TDU and are updated periodically (typically twice a year) with PUCT approval. q: Is the TDU delivery charge a scam or a hidden fee?
a: No. It is a legitimate, regulated cost approved by the PUCT, and every Texas customer on a competitive plan pays it. It is not something your retail provider profits from or can waive.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained
q: Can I shop around for a cheaper delivery charge?
a: No. Your TDU is assigned by location and cannot be changed by switching providers. What you can shop for is the energy rate and plan terms, which is exactly where choosing the right plan saves money.

t: TDU Delivery Charges in Texas, Explained

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label
q: Where do I find a Texas electricity plan's EFL?
a: Every retail electric provider in Texas must make the Electricity Facts Label available before you enroll, usually as a link or PDF next to the plan price during sign-up. If you cannot find it, ask for it. You are entitled to see it before you commit.

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label
q: Why does the average price on an EFL change at different usage levels?
a: Because fixed monthly charges and usage credits are spread across however many kWh you use. At low usage a flat monthly fee raises the average price per kWh; at higher usage a credit can lower it. Read the column nearest your real monthly usage, shown at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh.

What changes between plans is the energy charge, monthly fees, and credits the provider adds, and whether the EFL quotes delivery as included or separate.

a: If you only read one document before signing up for a Texas electricity plan, make it the Electricity Facts Label. A flashy advertised rate tells you almost nothing on its own. t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label
q: What is the EFL, and why does it matter?
a: The Public Utility Commission of Texas requires every retail electric provider to publish an Electricity Facts Label for each plan they sell. Think of it like the nutrition label on a food package: a single, consistent format so you can compare any two plans side by side instead of guessing which "low" rate is actually low.

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label
q: What is the EFL, and why does it matter?
a: The EFL exists because the advertised price is rarely the whole story. Two plans can both advertise an attractive headline rate and still cost very different amounts once delivery fees, monthly charges, and usage credits are factored in. The EFL forces all of that into the open.

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label

a: Texas EFLs follow the same structure, so once you know the four sections you can read any provider's label in under a minute.

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label

a: This is also where you confirm whether the rate is fixed or how charges are layered, which tells you how the bill behaves as your usage swings season to season.

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label

a: The EFL discloses whether TDU charges are already baked into the quoted price or added on top. This matters a lot when comparing plans, because a rate that looks cheaper may simply be quoting the energy charge alone and adding delivery later.

t: How to Read an Electricity Facts Label

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: Does ERCOT set my electricity rate?
a: No. ERCOT operates the grid and the wholesale market, but it does not set retail rates or send you a bill. In the deregulated parts of Texas, your retail electricity provider sets your plan price, and you choose that provider.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: Why isn't the Texas grid connected to the rest of the country?
a: Most of Texas runs on its own grid, the Texas Interconnection, which stays largely within state lines. Because it does not move large amounts of power across state borders, it mostly avoids federal interstate regulation, which lets Texas keep control over how its grid is managed. There are only limited connections to neighboring grids.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: Is all of Texas on the ERCOT grid?
a: No. ERCOT covers about 90 percent of the state's electric load. A few regions, including parts of El Paso, the upper Panhandle, and some of deep East Texas, are served by neighboring grids instead.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?

a: Indirectly. When demand surges in extreme weather, wholesale prices in the ERCOT market can rise, and retail providers factor that risk into their plans. t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: What ERCOT actually is
a: ERCOT stands for the Electric Reliability Council of Texas . It is a nonprofit organization that operates the electric grid for most of the state. ERCOT does not own power plants, poles, or wires, and it does not send you a bill. Its job is to manage the flow of electricity, making sure that the amount of power being generated matches the

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: What ERCOT actually is
a: Think of ERCOT as an air-traffic controller for electricity. Power plants produce energy, transmission lines carry it, and homes and businesses use it. ERCOT coordinates all of that in real time so the lights stay on. It also runs the wholesale market where electricity is bought and sold, and it oversees grid reliability for its region.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: What ERCOT actually is
a: ERCOT manages the grid for about 90 percent of the state's electric load and serves the large majority of Texans. A handful of areas, including parts of El Paso, the upper Panhandle, and some of deep East Texas, sit on neighboring grids instead.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: Why Texas has its own grid
a: The continental United States is divided into a small number of large power grids, often called interconnections. Most states belong to the Eastern Interconnection or the Western Interconnection . Texas is the major exception. Most of the state runs on its own grid, the Texas Interconnection , which ERCOT operates.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: Why Texas has its own grid
a: The reason is largely historical and legal. Texas utilities deliberately kept their grid contained within state lines. Because the Texas grid does not move large amounts of power across state borders, it stays mostly outside the jurisdiction of federal interstate regulation. t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?
q: Why Texas has its own grid
a: The trade-off is independence in both directions. Texas has more freedom to set its own rules, but it also has limited connections to neighboring grids, so it cannot easily import large amounts of electricity from other states during an emergency.

t: What Is ERCOT, and Why Does Texas Have Its Own Power Grid?

a: ERCOT runs the grid, but it does not pick your plan or set your retail rate. In deregulated Texas, you choose your own electricity provider, which is exactly where a little comparison shopping can pay off.

q: How ERCOT connects to deregulation
a: Most of the Texas ERCOT region is also deregulated , which is a separate idea that often gets tangled together with ERCOT. Deregulation means the company that delivers power to your home is separate from the company you buy your plan from. Generators produce the electricity and sell it into the ERCOT wholesale market.

Your retail provider is the company whose name is on your bill and whose plan you signed up for.

t: Why Texas Electricity Rates Vary by ZIP Code
q: Why do Texas electricity rates vary by ZIP code?
a: Rates vary by ZIP code because your ZIP determines your Transmission and Distribution Utility (TDU) and which retail providers serve your area. Different service territories have different delivery costs, demand levels, and competition, which all change your rate.

t: Why Texas Electricity Rates Vary by ZIP Code
q: Can two homes on the same street have different electricity rates?
a: Yes. Two identical homes on the same block can have different rates because their service addresses may fall into different delivery zones or have different providers competing for their business.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Why is my Texas electricity bill higher in winter than summer?
a: Usually because of heating. If your home uses an electric furnace, heat pump backup, or strip heat, a few cold days can drive far more kWh than a typical summer cooling day. Shorter days and more time indoors add a little more.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Does a fixed-rate plan protect me during a winter storm?
a: A fixed-rate plan locks your price per kWh, so a wholesale market spike during a freeze does not change your rate. Your bill can still rise if you use more electricity, but you are protected from the price spikes that hit variable and indexed plans.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: How do I know how much winter will cost me?
a: Check your plan's Electricity Facts Label (EFL) for the price at 1,000 and 2,000 kWh, then estimate your winter usage. Because plans often price differently at higher usage, your winter cost can differ from your summer cost on the same plan. Always confirm against the live EFL for your ZIP.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Should I switch plans before winter?
a: If your contract is ending or you are on a variable rate, comparing before a cold front is smart. A fixed-rate plan can give you a predictable price through the season. Energy Direct can compare options by your ZIP for free, and Ambit Energy handles the switch.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Why winter bills can climb in Texas
a: Cold snaps are short but intense. Texas does not stay cold for months, but a few sub-freezing days can drive your heating system to run almost continuously. Days are shorter. Lights, holiday decorations, and more time spent indoors all add a little to the baseline.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: The heat-pump and strip-heat factor
a: How your home heats matters more than almost anything else on a winter bill.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Heat pumps
a: Many Texas homes use heat pumps, which are efficient because they move heat rather than create it. The catch is that when outdoor temperatures drop near or below freezing, a heat pump loses efficiency and often switches to backup electric heat to keep up. That backup is where the usage spike comes from.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Electric resistance (strip) heat
a: Older or all-electric homes may rely on resistance strip heat or electric baseboards. These are essentially giant toasters and use a large amount of electricity per hour. A few days of running them hard can noticeably change a monthly bill.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Gas furnaces
a: If you heat with natural gas, most of your heating cost shows up on your gas bill, not your electricity bill. Your electricity use still rises a bit from the furnace blower and shorter days, but usually far less than an all-electric home.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Key insight
a: The single biggest winter swing on most Texas electric bills is backup electric heat kicking in during a cold snap. Knowing how your home heats tells you how exposed your bill is to a freeze.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: How the ERCOT grid affects cold-weather pricing
a: Most Texans buy power on the deregulated market run through ERCOT, the grid operator for most of the state. During extreme cold, demand surges and wholesale prices can spike sharply for short periods. What this means for you depends entirely on the type of plan you are on. Fixed-rate plans lock your price per kWh for the contract term, so

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: How the ERCOT grid affects cold-weather pricing
a: Variable-rate and indexed plans can move with the market. In a severe cold event, a plan tied to wholesale or "spot" pricing can produce a shockingly high bill. This is the kind of plan that caught some Texans off guard during major winter storms. For most homeowners who want a predictable winter, a fixed-rate plan is the safer structure.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Read your bill the right way: usage vs. rate
a: When a winter bill looks high, the first question is whether you used more or paid a higher rate. Pull up the bill and look at two numbers: kWh used this month vs. last winter. A big jump usually points to heating, not your provider. Your average price per kWh. Compare it to what your plan's Electricity Facts Label (EFL) says you should p

q: Read your bill the right way: usage vs. rate
a: Every Texas retail plan comes with an EFL that spells out the energy charge, the TDU delivery charges, any monthly base fees, and how the price changes at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh. Because many plans price differently at different usage levels, a low summer bill and a high winter bill can come from the very same plan. Always check the li

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Ways to keep winter bills down
a: Set the thermostat lower and steady. Each degree adds up, and a steady setting keeps backup heat from cycling on. Use a smart or programmable thermostat to ease back overnight and while you are out. Seal drafts. Weatherstripping doors, sealing windows, and adding attic insulation cut how hard your system has to work.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Ways to keep winter bills down
a: Change your air filter. A clogged filter makes the system run longer. Watch the forecast before a freeze. Knowing a cold snap is coming lets you prep the home and avoid surprises. Make sure you are on the right plan. If you are on a variable or expiring contract heading into winter, that is exactly when a fixed-rate plan can protect you.

t: Winter Electricity Bills in Texas: What to Expect
q: Compare your options before the cold hits
a: The best time to lock in a sensible winter plan is before a cold front, not after a high bill. If your contract is ending, you have rolled onto a month-to-month variable rate, or you simply have not compared in a while, it is worth seeing what is available at your address. a: If your contract is ending or you are on a variable rate, comparing before a cold front is smart. A fixed-rate plan can give you a predictable price through the season. Energy Direct can compare options by your ZIP for free.

q: What are TDU delivery charges?
a: TDU delivery charges are fees from your local Transmission and Distribution Utility for maintaining power lines, poles, and meters. They are the same for everyone in your area, no matter which retail provider you choose.

q: What is the difference between a REP and a TDU?
a: A REP, or Retail Electric Provider, sells you electricity plans and handles billing. A TDU, or Transmission and Distribution Utility, delivers the electricity and maintains the grid. Both appear on your bill, but only the REP can change your plan or rate.

q: Why do I see both delivery and energy charges on my bill?
a: The energy charge is what you pay your REP for usage. The delivery charge is from the TDU to cover infrastructure. Together they make up your total bill.

q: Can I lower my TDU delivery charges by switching providers?
a: No. Delivery charges are set by the utility and are identical across providers. Switching changes only the energy charge and plan terms, which is the part worth comparing.

q: REP vs TDU: who does what
a: In deregulated Texas, the company that keeps your power flowing is not the company you buy your plan from. Knowing the split makes your bill far easier to read.

q: TDU: Transmission and Distribution Utility
a: The TDU is the regulated local utility, such as AEP Texas in the Corpus Christi area, Oncor in North and West Texas, or CenterPoint in the Houston area. It maintains the poles, wires, and meters and restores outages. Delivers the electricity to your meter. Sets regulated delivery charges that apply to every provider equally.

q: TDU: Transmission and Distribution Utility
a: Handles outages no matter which retail plan you choose.

q: REP: Retail Electric Provider
a: The REP is the company you sign up with, such as Ambit Energy. It does not own the wires. It sets the energy charge and manages your account. Sells you the plan and the energy charge. Handles billing and customer service. Is the only party that can change your plan or rate.

q: How the charges show up on your bill
a: Most Texas bills bundle both companies into one statement, then break the charges into separate line items.

q: TDU delivery charges
a: A per-kWh delivery charge that scales with how much power you use. A fixed monthly delivery charge in many areas, regardless of usage. Identical across providers: these are set by the utility, so switching REPs does not change them.

q: REP energy charges
a: The energy charge from the plan you chose, which is the part you control by shopping. Any plan-specific items like base charges, minimum-usage fees, or bill credits, all spelled out in the EFL. This is where comparing plans pays off , since the delivery side is fixed.

q: What is a solar buyback plan?
a: It is a retail electricity plan that credits you for the excess solar energy your home exports to the grid. You still draw power from the grid at night or on cloudy days, and the plan sets how that energy is billed and how your exports are credited.

q: Do I need rooftop solar already installed for a solar buyback plan?
a: Yes. A solar buyback plan credits the energy your panels send back to the grid, so it is for homes that already have rooftop solar. If you are still planning your system, you can compare standard plans now and switch when your panels are online.

q: Will switching to a solar plan interrupt my power?
a: No. Your local utility keeps delivering power without interruption while your new plan begins billing. Your panels and your meter keep working exactly as before.

q: Plans built for solar homes
a: Ambit offers solar buyback plans that credit you for the excess power your panels export, so the energy you do not use still works for you.

q: How solar buyback works
a: You generate power: your rooftop panels produce electricity for your home during the day. You use what you need: your home draws from your panels first. You export the excess: extra power your home does not use flows back onto the grid. The plan credits you: a solar buyback plan credits you for that exported energy on your bill.

q: How solar buyback works
a: Your panels and your retail plan are two separate things. The plan sets how your exported energy is credited and how you are billed for the power you pull from the grid at night or on cloudy days.

q: What to compare on a solar plan
a: Buyback structure: how the plan credits the energy you export back to the grid. Export caps: whether credits are limited to a certain amount of exported energy. Term and early-termination fee: the contract length and what leaving early costs. Monthly and minimum-usage fees: any base charges or fees if you draw very little from the grid.

q: What to compare on a solar plan
a: Renewable content: the green percentage, if it matters to you. Plans and availability vary by ZIP, so a smart comparison starts with your address, not a single statewide number. Always read a plan's Electricity Facts Label before you enroll.

q: What is an Electricity Facts Label (EFL)?
a: It is a standardized, one-page disclosure that every Texas retail electricity provider must give you for each plan. It shows the average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh, the fees, the contract term, the early-termination fee, and the renewable content, so you can compare plans on the same terms.

q: Why does the EFL show three different prices?
a: Because your real price per kWh changes with how much electricity you use. Fixed monthly fees and bill credits get spread over more or fewer kilowatt-hours, so the average price at 500 kWh, 1,000 kWh, and 2,000 kWh is usually different for the same plan. Compare at the usage level that matches your home.

q: Why is my real bill higher than the advertised electricity rate?
a: Most often it is a bill credit you just missed. If a plan only gives the credit at 1,000 kWh or more and you use 999, you lose it entirely and your effective rate jumps. Minimum-usage fees, base monthly fees, and TDU delivery charges left out of an ad can also push your real cost above the headline rate.

q: What is the 1,000 kWh benchmark for comparing Texas plans?
a: It is the common point everyone uses to compare Texas plans, because a typical home uses around 1,000 kWh a month. It is a useful starting comparison, but if your usage is much higher or lower, compare plans at your own usage level instead.

q: What the Electricity Facts Label is
a: In Texas, every retail electricity provider has to give you an Electricity Facts Label , or EFL, for each plan it sells. Think of it as the nutrition label on a box of food: the marketing on the front of the box can say whatever it wants, but the label on the back has to tell the truth in a fixed, standardized format. Because the Public U

q: What the Electricity Facts Label is
a: The EFL is usually one page. It packs in the average price you will actually pay, the fees buried underneath the headline rate, the length of the contract, what it costs to leave early, and how much of the power is renewable. Reading it for two minutes before you sign is the single best way to avoid a bill that is much higher than the rat

q: The one habit that protects you
a: Never choose a plan from an advertised "cents per kWh" headline alone. Pull the EFL, find the average price at the usage level that matches your home, and compare that number against other plans. The headline rate and the real average price are often very different things.

q: Average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh
a: The most important box on the EFL shows the plan's average price per kWh at three usage levels: 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kilowatt-hours per month . The reason there are three numbers, instead of one, is that your true per-kWh price changes depending on how much electricity you use. Fixed monthly fees and bill credits get spread across more o

q: Average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh
a: Here is an illustration of how a single plan can show three very different average prices. These figures are an example to show the pattern, not a quote for any specific plan — always read the numbers on the actual EFL. Why it moves: a flat monthly fee is a big share of a small 500 kWh bill but a small share of a large 2,000 kWh bill, so

q: Average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh
a: The takeaway: find your own usage on a recent bill, then compare plans at that usage level. A plan that looks cheap at 2,000 kWh can be expensive for a small apartment that only uses 500 kWh, and vice versa.

q: Energy charge vs. Transmission and Distribution Utility (TDU) delivery charges
a: Your bill is built from two very different kinds of charges, and the EFL lists both:

q: The energy charge
a: This is what your retail provider charges for the electricity itself, set by the plan you choose. It is the part the provider actually competes on, and the part you are shopping for when you compare plans.

q: TDU delivery charges
a: The Transmission and Distribution Utility (TDU) owns the poles, wires, and meter and delivers power to your home. Its charges — a fixed monthly fee plus a per-kWh delivery rate — are set by the TDU, not your provider, and are roughly the same no matter which retailer you pick. A trustworthy EFL folds these into the average price so the nu

q: TDU delivery charges
a: Be cautious of any advertised rate that quietly leaves the TDU delivery charges out. The average price on the EFL is meant to include them, which is exactly why the EFL average is the honest number to compare and the front-of-the-ad rate often is not.

q: Base fees, minimum-usage fees, and bill credits
a: Several common EFL line items can quietly raise or lower what you really pay: Base charge / monthly service fee. A flat dollar amount added every month no matter how little power you use. On a small bill this fee can dominate, which is why light users often pay a high average price per kWh. Minimum-usage fee. Some plans charge an extra fe

q: Base fees, minimum-usage fees, and bill credits
a: Bill credit. A flat credit (for example, a set dollar amount off) that applies only when your usage lands in a specific range, such as at or above 1,000 kWh. Used to pull down the advertised average price at the benchmark usage level.

q: The danger of just missing a bill-credit threshold
a: Bill credits are the most common reason a real bill blows past the advertised rate. If a plan gives you a credit only when you use 1,000 kWh or more, then using 999 kWh means you lose the entire credit. A mild month where you fall just short of the threshold can make your effective price per kWh jump well above what the EFL's 1,000 kWh av

q: Contract length & early-termination fee
a: Term. The EFL states the contract length, commonly 12, 24, or 36 months. A fixed-rate term holds your energy price steady for the whole period. Early-termination fee (ETF). What it costs to leave before the term ends. If there is a real chance you will move or want to switch, weigh the ETF carefully — it can erase the savings from a low r

q: Contract length & early-termination fee
a: Renewal note. When your term ends, plans often roll to a variable month-to-month rate unless you pick a new plan, so mark your end date.

q: Renewable content
a: Renewable percentage. The EFL shows what share of the plan's power comes from renewable sources, from 0% up to 100% green plans. Why it is here. If supporting wind or solar matters to you, this line lets you compare the green percentage between plans directly. Reality check. A higher renewable percentage does not always cost more, so it i

q: The 1,000 kWh benchmark and reading a low rate critically
a: When you compare Texas plans, the 1,000 kWh average price is the most widely used benchmark, because a typical Texas home lands near that usage. It gives you a fair, common point of comparison across providers. But the benchmark is only a starting point — if your home consistently uses far more or far less than 1,000 kWh, compare at the l

q: The 1,000 kWh benchmark and reading a low rate critically
a: Here is how to read a low advertised rate critically, using nothing but the EFL: Find the average price at your real usage, not just the eye-catching number on the ad. Check whether the low average depends on a bill credit and exactly where that credit kicks in. Look for minimum-usage and base fees that punish light months.

q: The 1,000 kWh benchmark and reading a low rate critically
a: Confirm the rate is fixed, so it cannot drift upward mid-term, and note the contract length. Read the early-termination fee before you commit, in case your plans change. Do those five things and the EFL stops being fine print and becomes the tool that protects you. The plan with the lowest headline rate is frequently not the cheapest plan

q: Electricity Facts Label questions
a: It is a standardized, one-page disclosure that every Texas retail electricity provider must give you for each plan. It shows the average price at 500, 1,000, and 2,000 kWh, the fees, the contract term, the early-termination fee, and the renewable content, so you can compare plans on the same terms. q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms

EFL (Electricity Facts Label) A standardized document that summarizes a plan's key details: term length, energy charges, delivery charges, fees, and disclosures.

q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms
a: REP (Retail Electric Provider) A company that sells electricity plans to customers in deregulated markets. Transmission and Distribution Utility (TDU) (Transmission & Distribution Utility) The utility that maintains poles, wires, and meters and restores power after outages. ERCOT The Electric Reliability Council of Texas; operates the ele

q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms
a: Fixed-rate plan A plan where the energy charge is intended to remain the same during the contract term (see EFL). Variable plan A plan where the energy charge can change monthly (see EFL). q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms

q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms

Move-in (start service) Begin service at a new address. Transfer Move an existing plan to a new address when eligible.

q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms

q: Glossary of Texas electricity terms

q: Before you enroll
a: When comparing plans, always open the EFL and Terms of Service. Review the delivery charges, contract term, any base or minimum-usage fees, and the usage level where bill credits apply before you choose.

q: How do I know if I live in a deregulated area in Texas?
a: If you live in cities like Houston, Dallas, Fort Worth, Corpus Christi, Lubbock, Midland, Odessa, Abilene, or many surrounding suburbs, you are likely in a deregulated electricity area where you can choose your retail electricity provider.

t: Texas Electricity Guide: Compare Plans by ZIP
q: Does my electricity get disconnected when I switch providers in Texas?
a: No. Switching providers in deregulated Texas is designed to be seamless. As long as your account is in good standing, your power stays on while the switch is processed.

t: Texas Electricity Guide: Compare Plans by ZIP
q: Do I choose my TDU in Texas?
a: No. Your Transmission and Distribution Utility (TDU) is assigned based on your location. You can choose your retail electricity provider, but not the TDU that maintains the poles and wires in your area.

t: Texas Electricity Guide: Compare Plans by ZIP
q: Are delivery charges the same no matter which provider I choose?
a: Yes. TDU delivery charges are regulated and are the same across all retail electricity providers in that TDU service area. Those charges appear as a separate line item on your bill regardless of which supplier you choose.

t: Texas Electricity Guide: Compare Plans by ZIP
q: What contract length is best for a Texas electricity plan?
a: Most Texans choose a 12-month fixed-rate plan because it balances rate stability with flexibility. Longer terms like 24 or 36 months can be a good fit when market prices are low and you want long-term price protection.

q: How Texas got the power to choose
a: For most of the last century, one utility owned everything from the power plant to your meter, and you had no say in who sold you electricity. That changed at the turn of the century.

q: Senate Bill 7 and the 2002 opening
a: In 1999, Governor George W. Bush signed Senate Bill 7 , the law that broke up the old monopoly and ordered Texas to open electricity to retail competition. On January 1, 2002 , the market opened: for the first time, most Texas homes and businesses could choose their own retail electric provider, while the local wires company kept deliveri

q: Senate Bill 7 and the 2002 opening
a: The law split the business into three roles - the companies that generate power, the regulated utility (TDU) that delivers it, and the retail providers like Ambit that compete to sell it to you.

q: ERCOT, the PUCT, and your protections

Senate Bill 7 also locked in consumer protections every provider must honor: Safe, reliable, and reasonably priced electricity

q: Why Texas runs its own grid
a: Texas is the only state with its own self-contained power grid, and that independence is the reason it can run a competitive market the way it does.

t: Texas Electricity Guide: Compare Plans by ZIP
q: A grid built to stay inside Texas
a: When the federal government gained authority over interstate electricity sales under the Federal Power Act of 1935 , Texas utilities made a deliberate choice: keep the power inside state lines. If electricity never crosses a state border, it is not interstate commerce, so the Texas grid largely sits outside federal (FERC) jurisdiction. Ut

t: Texas Electricity Guide: Compare Plans by ZIP
q: One of only three grids in the country
a: The lower 48 states run on three grids: the Eastern Interconnection, the Western Interconnection, and ERCOT , which covers about 90% of Texas and ties to its neighbors only through a few small direct-current connections. That self-reliance is what lets Texas write its own market rules, including the retail competition that lets you choose

q: One of only three grids in the country

q: Will my lights go out if I switch providers in Texas City?
a: No. TNMP continues electric delivery without interruption while your new plan begins billing.

q: Who is the TDU in Texas City?
a: Texas-New Mexico Power (TNMP) is the Transmission and Distribution Utility for Texas City, managing lines, outages, and delivery.

q: Is Texas City part of the deregulated Texas electricity market?
a: Yes. Texas City is in the deregulated ERCOT retail-choice market, so residents can choose their own retail electric provider and plan while TNMP delivers the power.
